root: ^Night has passed. The garden wakes to birdsong and the soft rustle of leaves in a breeze that carries the scent of jasmine and cedar. Dew clings to every petal, every blade of grass, catching the first light and scattering it into tiny rainbows.

^Satan has not slept. He has spent the night moving through the garden in borrowed forms — a lion prowling the eastern meadows, a tiger crouching by the river, a cormorant perched on the Tree of Life. Watching. Learning. Searching for the shape that will serve his purpose.
^And now, in the grey hour before full dawn, he finds it.

^A serpent lies coiled at the base of a fig tree. It is beautiful — its scales gleam with an iridescent sheen of green and gold, its body moves with a liquid grace that is almost hypnotic. In Eden, where all creatures are innocent, the serpent is the most subtle of them all.
^Satan flows into the sleeping serpent like water into a vessel. The creature stirs, lifts its elegant head, and opens eyes that are no longer its own.
^(Satan relishes the disguise — there is craft in this, and he was always a craftsman.)
^(Satan loathes the indignity — an archangel reduced to crawling on his belly.)
^(Satan feels nothing. The serpent is a tool, nothing more.)

c-0: ^There is an art to deception, and Satan is the first artist. He flexes the serpent's coils, testing the body — the way it moves, the way it senses heat and vibration, the way its forked tongue tastes the air. Every muscle is a new instrument. Every scale is armour.

^He has been a warrior, a king, a diplomat. Now he will be something new: a tempter. And the serpent's form suits the role perfectly — beautiful enough to fascinate, strange enough to intrigue, subtle enough to speak without seeming to threaten.
^Satan-as-serpent rises from the grass and begins to move through the garden with sinuous, purposeful grace.
c-1: ^This. This is what he has been reduced to. Satan, Prince of the Morning, Sovereign of Pandemonium, Lord of the Fallen Host — inhabiting a worm. Slithering through wet grass. Tasting the air with a forked tongue.
^The humiliation burns worse than the Lake of Fire. But Satan swallows it, as he has swallowed so much else. Pride is a luxury he will reclaim later, when the work is done.
^For now, he is a serpent. And the serpent has a garden to cross.
c-2: ^The serpent's body responds to Satan's will as readily as his own. He moves through the grass, testing the form — efficient, low to the ground, nearly silent. The garden's creatures pay him no mind. In Eden, all beasts are neighbours.
^That innocence is the best camouflage Satan could ask for. No creature here knows what predation means. No creature here flinches at the approach of another.
^Satan winds his way through the undergrowth, heading for the place where he knows Eve walks alone each morning.

the_approach: ^The morning brightens. In the bower, Adam and Eve rise with the sun. They pray — that artless, devastating prayer that cuts Satan like a blade — and then they part for the morning's work.

^This is the moment Satan has been waiting for. Eve has argued, gently, that they might accomplish more by working apart — she among the roses, Adam among the fruit trees. Adam resisted, worried, sensing perhaps some premonition he could not name. But Eve persuaded him. She is, after all, free. God made her free.
^That freedom is what will destroy her.

^Eve moves through the garden alone, tending the roses that have grown wild overnight. She works with quiet pleasure, her hands moving among the thorns without hesitation. The roses do not prick her — not yet. In Eden, nothing hurts.
^Satan watches from the cover of a flowering hedge. The serpent's tongue flickers, tasting her presence on the air — warm skin, crushed petals, the faint sweetness of the morning's fruit.

^The serpent emerges from the hedge with deliberate grace. It does not slither — it glides, holding its head high, its crest fanned like a peacock's display, its scales catching the light and throwing it back in prismatic flashes.
^Eve notices. She pauses, a rose stem in her hand, and watches the beautiful creature wind its way toward her.
^(Satan approaches with flattery — Eve's beauty as the gateway to her vanity.)
^(Satan approaches with wonder — pretending to be amazed by her, drawing out her curiosity.)
^(Satan approaches with a question — the most dangerous weapon of all.)

c-0: ^The serpent stops a few paces from Eve. It rises, swaying, until its head is level with her waist. When it speaks, its voice is musical, honeyed, resonant — nothing like the hiss one might expect.
^Wonder not, sovereign mistress, if perhaps thy coming I have watched with such delight — who could behold thy radiance and not adore? All things that live look on thee with admiration.
^Eve startles. She has never heard an animal speak.
^How is this? You speak — with voice as sweet as from a tree of fairest fruit. What art thou, creature? In all this garden, no beast has ever addressed me so.
^The serpent bows its head — a gesture of submission that costs Satan nothing and gains him everything.
c-1: ^The serpent stops before Eve and rises slowly, its eyes wide with what appears to be genuine astonishment. It looks at her as though seeing something miraculous for the first time.
^What... what manner of being art thou? I have travelled through all this garden — I have seen the beasts, the birds, the shining fish — and nothing, nothing is like thee.
^Eve steps back, surprised. A speaking serpent. In all her days in Eden, she has never encountered this.
^You speak! But no creature in this garden has ever spoken to me. How comes this gift to you?
^The serpent tilts its head, as though the question delights it beyond measure.
c-2: ^The serpent approaches and speaks without preamble, as though continuing a conversation already begun.
^Tell me, bright creature — is it true that God has said you shall not eat of every tree in the garden?
^The directness catches Eve off guard. She frowns — not in anger but in thought.
^We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden. But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God has said: you shall not eat of it, neither shall you touch it, lest you die.
^The serpent's eyes gleam. The prohibition — stated plainly, from her own lips. Now he has something to work with.

the_tree: ^The conversation continues. Satan steers it with the patience of a master fisherman, letting the line play out, never pulling too hard, always giving Eve the illusion that she is the one leading.

^He tells her a story — a lie wrapped in truth, as all the best lies are. The serpent, he says, was once a common beast. Mute. Crawling. No different from the others. But then it found a tree — a magnificent tree, laden with fruit of surpassing beauty — and it ate.

^And look what I have become. Speech, reason, thought — all from a single fruit. If such power lies in what was forbidden to me, a lowly beast, imagine what it might bestow on thee, who art already so far above my kind.

^Eve's eyes move, almost involuntarily, toward the centre of the garden. She knows which tree the serpent means. She has seen it every day of her life — its dark bark, its heavy fruit, its strange, alluring presence.
^That is the tree which God has forbidden. We are not to eat of it, nor even touch it.
^(Satan presses harder — challenge the prohibition directly.)
^(Satan uses sympathy — he understands how unfair it must seem.)
^(Satan retreats — let the idea work on its own. Plant the seed and withdraw.)

c-0: ^The serpent's voice drops to something low and conspiratorial.
^You shall not surely die. For God knows that in the day you eat of it, your eyes shall be opened, and you shall be as gods — knowing good and evil.
^The words land like stones in still water. Eve's lips part. Her eyes widen.
^As gods. Why else would He forbid it? Not to protect you — but to keep you from becoming His equal. The fruit is not death. It is knowledge. And knowledge, dear lady, is power.
^Eve says nothing. But her gaze has not moved from the tree.
c-1: ^The serpent's voice softens. It sounds almost tender — almost kind.

^I do not pretend to understand the ways of your Creator. But I confess — it seems strange to me. This garden, so full of gifts, so generous in every way... and yet this one denial. This one fruit, kept from you. Does it not make you wonder why?
^Eve is quiet for a long moment. When she speaks, her voice is careful.
^I have wondered. Adam says we should not question it — that God's command is reason enough. And he is right. I know he is right.
^Eve does not answer. She does not need to. The 'but' is written on her face as clearly as the morning light.
c-2: ^The serpent pauses. It seems to consider, then bows its head as if conceding a point.
^You are right, of course. The command of your Creator is not to be questioned. I, a mere beast, should not presume to understand His reasons. Forgive me.

^The serpent begins to turn away — slowly, gracefully, letting the morning light play across its iridescent scales. It has said enough. The idea is planted. The question — why is it forbidden? — now lives in Eve's mind like a splinter.
^The serpent pauses. Turns back. Its eyes are patient, expectant.
^You said the fruit gave you speech. Show me — show me this tree you found.
^Satan does not smile. Serpents cannot smile. But if they could, this one would.

the_moment: ^They walk together toward the centre of the garden — Eve and the serpent, woman and devil, innocence and its destroyer. The morning sun filters through the canopy above, casting patterns of light and shadow on the path.
^The Tree of Knowledge stands before them.

^It is beautiful. Eve has always known it was beautiful — she has passed it a thousand times, admired the dark lustre of its bark, the heavy pendulum of its fruit. But she has never stood this close. She has never really looked.

^The fruit glows. Not with heavenly light — not the fierce white radiance of the angels who guard the garden gates — but with a warm, intimate luminescence, like candlelight, like the flush of a cheek, like the last ember in a dying fire.
^Eve reaches out her hand. Her fingers hover inches from the nearest fruit.
^(She hesitates. The command of God echoes in her mind.)
^(She does not hesitate. Curiosity has already won.)
^(She turns to the serpent, wanting reassurance — one last push.)

c-0: ^Her hand trembles. The fruit is right there — warm, fragrant, almost pulsing with the promise of everything she does not know. But God's voice echoes in the garden of her memory: You shall not eat of it, lest you die.
^Die. She does not truly understand the word. In Eden, nothing has ever died. But Adam explained it once — an ending. A ceasing to be. The opposite of everything the garden is.
^And yet the serpent ate and did not die. The serpent ate and gained speech, and thought, and wonder.
^Eve's hand steadies. She looks at the fruit. She looks at the serpent. She looks at the sky, where God's presence is as constant and invisible as the air itself.
c-1: ^There is no hesitation. Eve has already decided — perhaps she decided the moment the serpent first spoke, perhaps she decided years ago when she first looked at the forbidden tree and felt that strange pull in her chest.

^Her fingers close around the fruit. It comes away from the branch with a soft sound — not a snap but a sigh, as if the tree has been waiting for this moment, as if the fruit was always meant to fall into this hand.
^It is warm in her palm. It smells of everything and nothing — of honey and rain and sunlight and something else, something she has no name for because it does not yet exist in her vocabulary.
^Knowledge. That is what it smells like. She just doesn't know the word yet.
c-2: ^Eve turns to the serpent. Her eyes are bright with something that is not quite fear and not quite desire but some new emotion that Eden has never seen before.
^If I eat — will I truly not die?

^The serpent meets her gaze. Satan looks through those borrowed eyes and speaks with absolute conviction — because he believes it, or because he needs to, or because the distinction no longer matters.
^Look at me. I ate. I live. I speak. I think. You will not die, bright creature. You will awaken.
^Eve nods. She turns back to the tree. Her hand moves to the fruit — slowly, deliberately, with the gravity of someone who knows, however dimly, that what she is about to do cannot be undone.

the_close: ^The serpent watches from the grass as Eve stands before the Tree of Knowledge, the fruit in her hand — or nearly in her hand, or about to be in her hand. The moment stretches. Time itself seems to hold its breath.
^Above, the sun has climbed to its zenith. The garden is flooded with light — merciless, golden, all-seeing light that illuminates everything and hides nothing.

^In Heaven, God watches. He has always been watching. The Son stands beside Him, already grieving for what is about to happen, already preparing for the sacrifice that will follow — an atonement planned before the foundation of the world.
^In Hell, Sin feels a tremor pass through the gates. Death lifts its shapeless head and sniffs the air. Something is coming. Something is about to change.
^And in the garden, in the centre of Paradise, under the branches of the forbidden tree, Eve makes the choice that will reshape creation.
^Satan watches. He does not blink. He does not breathe.
^The serpent has done its work. What happens next belongs to Book Nine.
